questions.stem: According to the lecture, what is a key benefit of using an LLM to generate software requirements?
options: It guarantees a complete and consistent set of requirements on the first attempt
It removes the need for stakeholders to review the requirements
It provides a useful starting point and can suggest ideas the engineer had not considered
It produces identical requirement lists on every run, ensuring stability
questions.explanation: The lecture frames LLM-generated requirements as a starting point: some will be added, deleted, or changed, but the model can surface new ideas and critique existing requirements. It does not guarantee completeness, and non-determinism means runs differ.

questions.stem: When choosing which OpenAI model to use, the lecture highlighted two primary considerations. What are they?
options: Fitness for the task you want to perform, and cost per input/output token
Release date and total number of parameters
Context window size and training-data cutoff date
Latency and availability of fine-tuning support
questions.explanation: The lecture lists exactly two considerations: whether the model fits the task (different models excel at different tasks) and cost, expressed per input and output token. Parameter counts and release dates were not the stated criteria.

questions.stem: Your application must send images to the model and receive output that strictly conforms to a JSON schema. Based on the lecture's comparison of OpenAI's two text-generation interfaces, which should you choose?
options: The Chat Completions API, because it is stable and widely supported
The Responses API, because it supports multimodal input and can enforce JSON-schema responses
Either interface, since both support images equally well
Neither — schema enforcement requires fine-tuning a custom model
questions.explanation: The Responses API is the unified next-generation interface supporting text, images and audio, advanced tool calling, and enforced JSON-schema responses. Chat Completions is stable but does not support multimodal input.

questions.stem: In an LLM API call, what distinguishes the system prompt from the user prompt?
options: The system prompt sets the model's role or behavior, while the user prompt contains the actual question or task
The user prompt configures the model's behavior, while the system prompt contains the question
The system prompt is sent once per account, while the user prompt is sent with every request
The system prompt sets the temperature, while the user prompt sets the maximum tokens
questions.explanation: The system prompt establishes the LLM's role and behavior (e.g., 'You are a requirements engineering expert'), and the user prompt carries the concrete task. Sampling parameters like temperature are set separately in the API call, not in either prompt.

questions.stem: Which three strategies were taught in class for combining multiple LLM responses to the same prompt?
options: Concatenation, deduplication, and ranking
Beam search, sampling, and greedy decoding
Weighted voting, plurality voting, and confidence scoring
Union, intersection, and majority voting
questions.explanation: The three response combiners taught are union (item in any list), intersection (item in all lists), and majority voting (item in more than half the lists). Weighted voting, plurality voting, and confidence scoring were mentioned only as fallbacks when no majority emerges.

questions.stem: Given n LLM responses L1..Ln, which rule defines the MAJORITY VOTING combiner for building a consolidated list L, as taught in the lecture?
options: An item is in L if it appears in at least one of the lists Li
An item is in L if it appears in every list Li
An item is in L if it appears in at least n/2 + 1 of the lists Li
An item is in L if it appears in at least n/3 of the lists Li
questions.explanation: Majority voting requires an item to appear on at least n/2 + 1 of the lists — a true majority. Appearing in at least one list is the union rule, and appearing in every list is the intersection rule.

questions.stem: You are brainstorming requirements for a new app and want the richest, most diverse candidate list from four LLM responses, accepting that some items may be low-confidence. Which response combiner BEST fits this goal?
options: Intersection, because it keeps only widely supported items
Union, because it includes any item that appears in at least one response
Majority voting, because it filters out one-off suggestions
Confidence scoring, because it ranks items by the model's log-probabilities
questions.explanation: Union provides the richest and most diverse set of answers, making it ideal for brainstorming and comprehensive coverage. Its known pitfall — including low-confidence items mentioned by only one model — is acceptable in this scenario.

questions.stem: A team specifying a safety-critical medical device wants a conservative requirements list containing only items that every one of its five LLM responses agrees on. Which combiner should they use?
options: Union, to make sure nothing important is dropped
Plurality voting, to break ties among the responses
Majority voting, to balance diversity and assurance
Intersection, since requiring an item on every list gives the most assurance it meets the prompt criterion
questions.explanation: Intersection admits an item only if it appears in all responses, giving the highest confidence that each retained item meets the criterion. Majority voting is more balanced but still admits items missing from some responses.

questions.stem: Three LLM responses to the same prompt yield the item sets {A,B,C,D}, {A,B,E}, and {A,C,F}. Which consolidated list does majority voting (item must appear in at least 2 of the 3 responses) produce?
options: {A, B}
{A, B, C}
{A, B, C, D, E, F}
questions.explanation: A appears in all three responses, and B and C each appear in two, so majority voting yields {A, B, C}. {A} alone is the intersection, and the full six-item set is the union.

questions.stem: You run majority voting over several LLM answers to a single-answer question, but no answer receives more than half the votes. Which fallback did the lecture describe as the most common practical choice?
options: Plurality voting — pick whichever answer got the most votes, even if under 50%
Weighted voting — weight each sample by a quality signal such as a verifier score
Confidence scoring — pick the response with the highest aggregate log-probability
Discard all answers and report that the question is unanswerable
questions.explanation: The lecture lists plurality voting as the most common practical fallback when no true majority emerges. Weighted voting, generating additional samples, and confidence/probability scoring are alternative fallbacks, not the default.

questions.stem: Which practice did the lecture recommend specifically to avoid even vote splits when using majority voting?
options: Set temperature to 0 so every sample is identical
Use an odd number of samples
Use the shortest response as the tiebreaker
Limit sampling to exactly two responses
questions.explanation: An odd number of samples avoids even splits. The lecture also suggested increasing (not zeroing) temperature to diversify outputs so a correct answer clusters distinctly, and chain-of-thought prompting to help reasoning converge — temperature 0 would defeat the purpose by making samples nearly identical.

questions.stem: You implement majority voting across 5 LLM calls. For a math problem with a numeric answer, a small script that counts matching answers works well, but for open-ended requirement lists it fails badly. Why?
options: The LLM returns items in a different order each time, and programs cannot compare unordered collections
Programmatic counting is only valid when the number of samples is odd
Non-determinism means there is nothing stable for a program to count
Free-form answers can be semantically equivalent yet worded differently, so exact matching miscounts votes — an LLM can recognize the equivalence instead
questions.explanation: A deterministic counting program gives the best accuracy for structured answers like numbers, but free-form text expresses the same idea in different words ('Macronutrients' vs. 'protein, carbs and fat'), so string matching miscounts. The lecture's solution is to hand the responses to an LLM, which can treat semantically equivalent answers as the same.

questions.stem: You ask an older LLM (e.g., GPT-3.5) to compute both the union and the intersection of three requirement lists, and the returned 'intersection' contains MORE items than the returned 'union'. What should you conclude?
options: The union prompt was under-specified, so the union output is wrong and the intersection is right
The model failed at the set operation — an intersection can never contain more items than the union of the same lists
Duplicate items in the original lists legitimately make the intersection larger
The temperature was too low, causing items to be dropped from the union
questions.explanation: By definition the intersection is a subset of the union, so this result is logically impossible and signals model error — exactly what happened with GPT-3.5 in the lecture, which returned a 15-item 'intersection' with duplicates. The fix is to use a newer model (GPT-4o-mini answered correctly) or implement the set logic in code.

questions.stem: How does Mixture-of-Agents (MoA) differ from simple majority voting across LLMs?
options: In MoA, LLMs collaborate — each sees other models' outputs and refines the answer over multiple layers — instead of voting independently in a single round
MoA requires every agent to be a distinct LLM from a different vendor
MoA selects the final answer by counting which response received the most votes across layers
MoA is cheaper because it needs fewer API calls than voting
questions.explanation: MoA's defining features are collaboration (each LLM is given the other LLMs' results) and multiple layers of refinement, with a final aggregate-and-synthesize step rather than vote counting. Notably, each 'agent' can be a different instance of the same LLM, and the layered calls make MoA more expensive, not cheaper.

questions.stem: Compared with single-round majority voting, what is a main drawback of the Mixture-of-Agents (MoA) approach?
options: It cannot combine more than two models
It reduces answer diversity to a single model's viewpoint
It is more expensive, since multiple layers of models must each process the other models' outputs
It requires labeled training data to weight each agent
questions.explanation: MoA replaces cheap independent calls and simple counting with collaborative, multi-layer synthesis, so it costs more compute and API calls. It actually preserves diversity by aggregating multiple models' contributions and needs no labeled training data.

questions.stem: According to the recitation, why might a developer prefer LangChain over raw OpenAI API calls?
options: It removes per-token charges by batching calls locally
It offers built-in output parsing, easy prompt chaining, built-in error handling, and one-line switching between model vendors
It makes LLM outputs fully deterministic regardless of temperature
It automatically trains custom models on your project data
questions.explanation: The recitation contrasts raw API usage (manual request setup, manual parsing, manual retries, vendor lock-in) with LangChain's built-in parsing, chaining, error handling, and model portability. LangChain does not change token pricing or model determinism.

questions.stem: Formally, given N requirements R = {r1..rN} and K predefined categories C = {c1..cK}, the requirements classification problem seeks ____.
options: a ranking of R ordered by implementation priority
a partition of C into functional and non-functional subsets
a mapping from C to the single most representative requirement in R
a mapping F: R × C → {True, False} indicating whether each requirement belongs to each category
questions.explanation: Classification is defined as a mapping from the Cartesian product R × C to {True, False}: F(ri, cj) = True means requirement ri belongs to category cj. Ranking by priority is a different RE task (prioritization), not classification.

questions.stem: What is the core distinction between functional and non-functional requirements?
options: Functional requirements describe what the system should do; non-functional requirements describe how well it should operate (performance, security, usability)
Functional requirements are mandatory, while non-functional requirements are optional
Functional requirements come from end users, while non-functional requirements come only from regulators
Functional requirements are testable, while non-functional requirements cannot be tested
questions.explanation: Functional requirements specify features and operations users can directly observe (login, search, payments), while non-functional requirements are quality attributes governing how well functions are performed (response time, scalability, security). Both kinds are binding and testable.

questions.stem: You are labeling requirements for a food-delivery app. Which of the following is a NON-FUNCTIONAL requirement?
options: Users can browse the menu and place an order
Users can track their orders in real time
The app should load the restaurant menu in under 1 second
Users can pay for their order with a credit card in the app
questions.explanation: A load-time constraint describes how well the system performs rather than a feature it provides, making it non-functional. Browsing, ordering, tracking, and paying are all observable system functions.

questions.stem: The Promise dataset labels 'Website must interface with the CyberSource API to process credit card transactions' as NF, yet Claude, ChatGPT and Wizard all labeled it F. Which reasoning supports the ground-truth NF label?
options: Payment processing is a quality attribute, like performance or security
The requirement constrains WHICH API implementation must be used, rather than describing how the user works with the system or a task it performs
Any requirement that names an external company is non-functional by definition
The requirement is ambiguous, and ambiguous requirements default to NF
questions.explanation: The counter-argument in the lecture is that this requirement dictates an implementation constraint (use CyberSource) rather than user-facing behavior, which is why the dataset labels it NF. The models' rationale — that it describes a payment-processing function — is plausible, illustrating that some labels are debatable.

questions.stem: The lecture noted that some F/NF labels in the Promise dataset are debatable and the dataset contains duplicates and mislabeled requirements. What broader lesson was drawn from this?
options: LLMs should never be evaluated against human-labeled datasets
Any dataset published before the model's training cutoff is unusable
Classification accuracy below 100% always indicates a badly written prompt
Some classification tasks are subjective — reasonable people (and models) can disagree, so labels are not always black-and-white
questions.explanation: The lecture distinguishes objective tasks with clear answers from subjective ones where reasonable people can differ; some F/NF distinctions fall in the latter category. This means imperfect scores may reflect debatable ground truth rather than pure model or prompt failure.

questions.stem: In the lecture's F/NF classification experiment, the LLM was told to answer only 'F' or 'NF' but often replied with sentences like 'This requirement is a functional requirement.' How did the instructor's program handle this?
options: Simple keyword heuristics inferred 'F' or 'NF' from the longer text, e.g., by searching for 'non-functional' first
The response was discarded and the API was called again at temperature 0
A JSON schema was enforced on the Chat Completions call to force a one-token answer
The requirement was skipped and automatically counted as misclassified
questions.explanation: The code searched the response for keywords such as 'non-functional', 'NF', 'functional' and 'F' (checking NF patterns first) and inferred the label; this always succeeded in the experiment. Schema-enforced output and constrained decoding were mentioned as future-lecture techniques, not what was used here.

questions.stem: A medical test reports that a patient does NOT have a disease, but the patient actually has it. In binary classification terms, this outcome is a ____.
options: false positive
true negative
false negative
true positive
questions.explanation: A false negative is when the model says 'not X' but the item really is X — here, the test misses a real disease. A false positive would be flagging a healthy patient as sick.

questions.stem: Let TP = true positives, FP = false positives, and FN = false negatives. Precision is defined as:
options: TP / (TP + FN)
TP / (TP + FP)
FP / (TP + FP)
TP / (TP + FP + FN)
questions.explanation: Precision = TP/(TP+FP): of everything the model labeled positive, how many really were positive. TP/(TP+FN) is recall — the tempting confusion — which measures how many of the actual positives were found.

questions.stem: When evaluating an F/NF classifier, which question does RECALL for the class F answer?
options: Of all the actual functional requirements, how many did the classifier correctly find?
Of everything the classifier labeled F, how many were truly functional?
What fraction of all predictions, across both classes, were correct?
How well balanced are the classifier's precision and its error rate?
questions.explanation: Recall = TP/(TP+FN) asks how much of the real positive class was captured. 'Of everything labeled F, how many were truly F' is precision, and the overall fraction correct is accuracy.

questions.stem: A dataset contains 12 images of dogs. A classifier labels 8 images as dogs, and 5 of those 8 actually contain dogs. Approximately what is the classifier's F1 score?
questions.explanation: Precision = 5/8 = 0.625 and recall = 5/12 ≈ 0.417, so F1 = 2 × (0.625 × 0.417)/(0.625 + 0.417) ≈ 0.499. Answering 0.62 or 0.42 confuses F1 with precision or recall alone; F1 is their harmonic mean.

questions.stem: You are building a screening classifier where missing a true case (a false negative) is far more costly than raising a false alarm — like medical screening for a disease. Which metric should you prioritize?
options: Precision
F1 score
Recall
Accuracy
questions.explanation: Use recall when false negatives are critical — a medical screen must not miss sick patients. Precision matters most when false positives are costly (e.g., a spam filter blocking real email), and F1 when the two are equally important.

questions.stem: A fraud detector predicts 'normal' for every one of 10,000 transactions (9,900 normal, 100 fraud) and achieves 99% accuracy. What does this example illustrate?
options: Accuracy is misleading on imbalanced data — the model catches zero fraud (recall = 0), so per-class metrics like recall and F1 are needed
The model is production-ready, since 99% exceeds typical benchmarks
The model's precision on fraud is 100%, so no further evaluation is needed
The dataset must be relabeled before any evaluation is meaningful
questions.explanation: With heavy class imbalance, a trivial always-'normal' model scores 99% accuracy while detecting no fraud at all. That is why precision, recall and F1 on the minority class are the meaningful measures of classifier quality here.

questions.stem: You classify 100 requirements. Of 40 functional ones, 32 are correctly labeled F; of 60 non-functional ones, 42 are correctly labeled NF. Using the lecture's per-class averaging, what is the overall recall?
questions.explanation: Recall-f = 32/40 = 0.80 and Recall-nf = 42/60 = 0.70, and the lecture combines them by simple averaging: (0.80 + 0.70)/2 = 0.75. Answering 0.74 computes the pooled fraction (74/100) instead of the average of the per-class recalls.

questions.stem: Why did the lecture call requirements PRIORITIZATION trickier for an LLM than checking requirements for consistency?
options: Prioritization requires numeric output, which LLMs cannot produce reliably
LLMs cannot compare more than two requirements at a time
Prioritization depends on business context the LLM is unaware of, such as a key customer demanding a feature to sign a large contract
Consistency checking never requires understanding the requirement text
questions.explanation: Prioritization hinges on external factors invisible to the model — the lecture's example is a key customer whose contract depends on a specific requirement. Consistency and conflict checking can be done from the requirement text itself.

questions.stem: In requirements traceability, which task did the lecture say an LLM can assist with?
options: Automatically deploying code that satisfies each requirement
Finding which lower-level requirements or software artifacts satisfy a given higher-level requirement
Encrypting the requirements document so only stakeholders can read it
Replacing the requirements document with comments in the source code
questions.explanation: Traceability links high-level requirements to the sub-requirements and software artifacts that fulfill them — e.g., 'data security' traced to 'data encrypted on disk' and 'https protocol used'. An LLM can be asked to discover these links; it does not deploy code.

questions.stem: According to the recitation, what behavior does setting temperature = 0 produce in an LLM?
options: Nearly deterministic output — the model almost always picks the most probable next token
Maximum creativity, since the model explores unlikely tokens
Uniform random sampling over the entire vocabulary
Shorter responses, because low temperature truncates the output
questions.explanation: At temperature 0 the model nearly always selects the most probable token, giving near-deterministic responses; around 0.7 it produces coherent creative variation, and at 1.0+ output is highly varied and sometimes incoherent. Temperature affects randomness, not response length or speed.

questions.stem: Which of the following was NOT presented in class as an advantage of majority voting over accepting a single LLM response?
options: Increased accuracy from aggregating multiple outputs
Robustness — one bad response does not ruin the result
Better generalization by capturing a broader range of knowledge
Faster processing speed
questions.explanation: Majority voting improves accuracy, robustness, and generalization, but it requires multiple LLM calls plus a combining step, so it is slower and more expensive than a single call — speed is not one of its advantages.

questions.stem: Prompts p1 and p2 ask the same question using different words. Based on the class discussion of LLM non-determinism, what can you say about the two responses?
options: Because the prompts mean the same thing, the LLM will return the same response
The responses are guaranteed to differ only slightly
Nothing is guaranteed — response similarity is not a predictable function of prompt similarity
Response similarity grows exactly in proportion to prompt similarity
questions.explanation: LLMs sample tokens from a probability distribution, so even the identical prompt can produce different responses, and rewording can shift outputs unpredictably. There is no guarantee of sameness, of only-slight difference, or of proportionality.

questions.stem: According to the recitation, how is the monetary cost of an LLM API call determined?
options: A flat fee is charged per API call, regardless of content
Cost is based on the number of input tokens plus output tokens processed
Cost is based on the wall-clock time the model spends generating
Only the model's output is billed; input tokens are free
questions.explanation: Every API call costs money based on input plus output tokens, which is also why model pricing is quoted per input and output token. Neither call count alone nor generation time determines the bill.

questions.stem: In HW1 Part 2, the 5 majority-voting calls for an article return: world, business, world, sci/tech, business. The ground-truth label is 'world'. How is this article scored?
options: It is recorded as 'no majority' and counted as incorrect, since no category received at least 3 of 5 votes
'world' wins as the plurality answer, so the article is counted correct
The tie between world and business is broken by the first call, so it is counted correct
It is counted correct because the ground-truth category appears among the votes
questions.explanation: HW1 defines the majority vote as a category with at least 3 of 5 votes; here world and business each get only 2, so the result is 'no majority'. The assignment states a 'no majority' result is always counted as incorrect, regardless of the ground truth.

questions.stem: In HW1, the LLM replies 'Based on the content, this seems to discuss the economy,' and your parser cannot map the reply to world, sports, business, or sci/tech. What should your program record as the extracted category for this call?
options: Nothing — skip this article entirely
Re-issue the API call in a loop until a mappable label is returned
The raw LLM sentence, so graders can judge it themselves
The label 'unknown'
questions.explanation: HW1 requires normalizing every raw response to one of the four lowercase category labels, and any response that cannot be mapped must produce 'unknown'. Writing raw text or skipping articles violates the required classifications.txt format.

questions.stem: HW1 warns about the effect of temperature on its 5-call majority voting. What should you expect as temperature increases?
options: Higher accuracy, because output diversity always improves voting
Identical answers across the 5 calls, making voting redundant
More 'no majority' outcomes as votes spread across categories, which can lower overall accuracy
Fewer output tokens per call, reducing the cost of the experiment
questions.explanation: Higher temperature makes sampling more random, so the 5 calls disagree more often and no category may reach the required 3 votes; HW1 counts every 'no majority' as incorrect, dragging accuracy down. Identical answers are what LOW temperature tends toward.

questions.stem: In HW1 Part 2 you must write three prompts using different techniques. What must the chain-of-thought prompt do that the zero-shot prompt does not?
options: Include exactly 4 labeled examples, one per news category
Explicitly instruct the LLM to reason step by step about category indicators before giving a final label — which your code must then parse from the longer response
Ask the model to answer with a single lowercase word and nothing else
Raise the temperature so the model's reasoning becomes more creative
questions.explanation: The CoT prompt must direct the model to reason step by step before answering, so the final category has to be extracted from a longer response. Including exactly 4 examples (one per category) is the requirement for the FEW-SHOT prompt, not chain-of-thought.

questions.stem: In HW1 Part 3, at your best temperature the program labels 10 articles as 'business'; 8 of those are truly business, and the dataset contains 12 actual business articles. What precision should statistics.txt report for the business category?
questions.explanation: Precision(c) = correctly classified as c / everything classified as c = 8/10 = 80.00. Answering 66.67 computes recall (8 of 12 actual business articles), and 72.73 is the F1 of these two values.
